title: Active Learning
description: Uncertainty sampling, diversity sampling, query-by-committee, expected model change, pool-based vs stream-based AL, and cold-start strategies.
details: Active Learning reduces labeling cost by letting the model choose which data points to label. Instead of labeling random data, the algorithm queries the most informative examples. This file covers uncertainty sampling (least confidence, margin, entropy), query-by-committee (vote entropy, KL divergence), expected model change, expected error reduction, diversity sampling (coreset, dissimilarity), pool-based vs stream-based active learning, cold-start strategies (random seed, unsupervised initialization), batch active learning (determinantal point processes, badge sampling), stopping criteria, and practical considerations (cold start, noisy oracles, variable labeling cost). Active Learning is especially relevant for applications where labels are expensive (medical imaging, legal document review, NLP annotation).

sections.title: Uncertainty Sampling & Query Strategies
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: The model doesn't know what it doesn't know — but it knows what it's unsure about. Query the examples where the model has the least confidence in its prediction. Labeling those examples provides the most information per label.

Uncertainty measures (for classifiers):

  • Least confidence: x* = argminx P(ŷ | x). Query the example where the top predicted class has the lowest probability. Simple but ignores the distribution among non-top classes.
  • Margin sampling: x* = argminx P(y1 | x) - P(y2 | x). Query where the difference between top two classes is smallest. More informative than least confidence — considers the second best class.
  • Entropy: x* = argmaxx -Σ P(yi | x) log P(yi | x). Query where the full distribution has highest entropy. Considers all classes. Most general uncertainty measure.

Query-by-Committee (QBC):

  • Train an ensemble of models (committee) on labeled data. Each committee member votes on unlabeled examples.
  • Query examples with maximum disagreement among committee members.
  • Vote entropy: x* = argmaxx -Σ (V(yi) / C) · log(V(yi) / C) where V(yi) = number of votes for class yi, C = committee size.
  • KL divergence: x* = argmaxx (1/C) Σ DKL(Pc || Pensemble). Average disagreement between each committee member and the consensus.

sections.title: Diversity Sampling & Batch AL
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Uncertainty sampling can pick redundant examples (all uncertain in the same region). Diversity sampling ensures the queried batch covers different regions of the feature space, maximizing information gain per batch.

Batch active learning strategies:

  • Coreset selection: Select a subset of unlabeled points whose removal minimizes the covering radius. Intuitively: pick points that are far from currently labeled data. The “maximin” problem — maximize the minimum distance between selected points.
  • BADGE (Batch Active learning by Diverse Gradient Embeddings): Compute gradient embeddings for each unlabeled point (gradient of the loss w.r.t. the final layer, evaluated at the predicted class). These embeddings capture both uncertainty (gradient magnitude) and diversity (gradient direction). Run k-means++ on gradient embeddings to select diverse batch.
  • Determinantal Point Processes (DPP): Sample a subset with probability proportional to det(KS), where KS is a similarity submatrix. DPP naturally encourages diversity — subsets with diverse items have higher determinant.

Practical considerations:

  • Cold start: With zero labels, uncertainty-based methods don't work. Solution: random seed + unsupervised pre-clustering to select initial diverse batch.
  • Noisy oracles: Real annotators make mistakes. Use repeated labeling, annotator agreement models, or learning from noisy labels (see Imbalanced Learning).
  • Variable cost: Not all labels cost the same. Cost-sensitive active learning: query x = argmax benefit(x) / cost(x).
  • Stopping criteria: Stop when model performance plateaus, uncertainty scores plateau, or labeling budget exhausted.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics import pairwise_distances
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans

# === Coreset selection (greedy maximin) ===
def greedy_coreset(X_labeled, X_unlabeled, n_query=10):
    """
    Select points farthest from currently labeled set.
    Uses the greedy k-center algorithm.
    """
    if len(X_labeled) == 0:
        # Cold start: pick random points
        return np.random.choice(len(X_unlabeled), n_query, replace=False)
    
    # Distance from each unlabeled point to nearest labeled point
    dists = pairwise_distances(X_unlabeled, X_labeled, metric='euclidean')
    min_dists = dists.min(axis=1)  # [n_unlabeled]
    
    selected = []
    for _ in range(n_query):
        # Pick the farthest unlabeled point
        idx = np.argmax(min_dists)
        selected.append(idx)
        
        # Update distances: min(d_current, dist to newly selected)
        new_dists = pairwise_distances(
            X_unlabeled, X_unlabeled[idx:idx+1], metric='euclidean'
        ).flatten()
        min_dists = np.minimum(min_dists, new_dists)
        min_dists[idx] = -1  # already selected
    
    return np.array(selected)

# === BADGE (Gradient Embeddings) ===
# For a model with softmax + cross-entropy:
# gradient embedding for point x_i = (p_i_hat - y_i_onehot) * phi(x_i)
# where phi(x_i) = penultimate layer activations
# p_i_hat = predicted probabilities, y_i_onehot = one-hot of predicted class

def badge_embeddings(model, X_unlabeled):
    """
    Compute gradient embeddings for batch diversity.
    Assumes model has .predict_proba and .get_penultimate (custom).
    """
    probas = model.predict_proba(X_unlabeled)
    pred_classes = np.argmax(probas, axis=1)
    n_classes = probas.shape[1]
    
    # Penultimate features (last hidden layer before softmax)
    phi = model.get_penultimate(X_unlabeled)  # [n, d]
    
    # Gradient embedding for each point
    # For cross-entropy: grad = (p_hat - y) * phi
    # Using predicted class as pseudo-label (y = one-hot of argmax)
    embeddings = np.zeros((len(X_unlabeled), phi.shape[1] * n_classes))
    for i in range(len(X_unlabeled)):
        g = probas[i].copy()
        g[pred_classes[i]] -= 1  # gradient w.r.t. logits
        # Outer product: [n_classes] x [d] = [n_classes * d]
        embeddings[i] = np.outer(g, phi[i]).flatten()
    
    return embeddings

def badge_query(model, X_unlabeled, n_query=10):
    """
    Run k-means++ initialization on gradient embeddings.
    Uses the initialization as diverse batch selection.
    """
    embeddings = badge_embeddings(model, X_unlabeled)
    
    # k-means++ initialization (diverse centroid selection)
    n = len(embeddings)
    indices = [np.random.choice(n)]
    
    for _ in range(n_query - 1):
        dists = pairwise_distances(embeddings, embeddings[indices])
        min_dists = dists.min(axis=1)
        probs = min_dists / min_dists.sum()
        idx = np.random.choice(n, p=probs)
        indices.append(idx)
    
    return np.array(indices)

# === DPP sampling (simplified) ===
def dpp_sample(similarity_matrix, k):
    """Sample k diverse items via DPP (greedy)."""
    n = similarity_matrix.shape[0]
    selected = []
    
    for _ in range(k):
        scores = []
        for i in range(n):
            if i in selected:
                continue
            candidate = selected + [i]
            # Determinant of similarity submatrix
            submatrix = similarity_matrix[np.ix_(candidate, candidate)]
            scores.append(np.linalg.det(submatrix))
        selected.append(np.argmax(scores))
    
    return np.array(selected)

sections.title: Expected Model Change & Error Reduction
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Instead of just looking at model uncertainty, estimate the expected impact of labeling a particular example. Will it change the model a lot? Will it reduce expected error on the unlabeled pool? These decision-theoretic strategies are more principled but computationally expensive.

Expected Model Change (EMC):

  • x* = argmaxx Σ P(y | x) · ||∇L(x,y)||. The expected change in the model after labeling x, under all possible labels weighted by their probability.
  • For gradient-based models: compute gradient of loss at (x, ŷ), weighted by confidence. Higher gradient magnitude = more informative.
  • Equivalent to BADGE in expectation (but BADGE uses predicted label only, not full distribution).

Expected Error Reduction (EER):

  • For each candidate x, estimate the expected reduction in generalization error if x were labeled and added to training set.
  • x* = argminx Σ P(y | x) · E[L(Dpool | Dtrain ∪ (x, y))].
  • Computationally expensive: requires retraining for each candidate. Typically approximated with:
    • Fisher information approximation: Use Fisher information matrix to approximate the change in model parameters.
    • Influence functions: Use influence from robust statistics to estimate effect of adding a training point without retraining.

Practical AL tip: Use uncertainty sampling for the first 70% of queries (fast, good enough). Switch to diversity-aware strategies for the last 30% (when uncertainty is low but coverage is incomplete). Hybrid strategies (e.g., uncertainty-weighted coresets) often work best.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from scipy.special import softmax

# === Expected Gradient Length ===
def expected_gradient_length(model, X_unlabeled):
    """
    Approximate expected model change for logistic regression.
    For neural nets: use penultimate layer representations.
    """
    probas = model.predict_proba(X_unlabeled)
    n_classes = probas.shape[1]
    n_features = X_unlabeled.shape[1]
    
    # Expected gradient norm for each candidate
    scores = []
    for i in range(len(X_unlabeled)):
        x = X_unlabeled[i:i+1]
        probs = probas[i]
        
        # Expected gradient: sum over all possible labels
        expected_grad_norm = 0
        for c in range(n_classes):
            # Gradient for logistic regression at (x, c)
            # L = -log(p_c), grad = (p - y) * x
            # Where y is one-hot
            y_onehot = np.eye(n_classes)[c]
            grad = (probs - y_onehot)  # [n_classes]
            grad_norm = np.linalg.norm(np.outer(grad, x.flatten()))
            expected_grad_norm += probs[c] * grad_norm
        
        scores.append(expected_grad_norm)
    
    return np.array(scores)

# === Influence Function-based selection ===
def influence_score(model, X_unlabeled, X_labeled, y_labeled):
    """
    Estimate effect of adding each unlabeled point (if labeled correctly)
    on the loss over the labeled set.
    
    Influence(x_new, x_i) = -grad(L(x_i))^T H^{-1} grad(L(x_new))
    
    x_new with high influence would change model predictions the most.
    Query the most influential unlabeled points.
    """
    # Simplified: use Cook's distance analog
    # For linear models: influence ~ (leverage * residual^2) / (1 - leverage)
    
    from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
    
    # Get training predictions
    y_pred = model.predict_proba(X_labeled)
    residuals = y_labeled - y_pred[:, 1]  # for binary
    
    # Hat matrix diagonal (leverage): X (X^T X)^{-1} X^T diag
    H = X_labeled @ np.linalg.inv(
        X_labeled.T @ X_labeled + 1e-6 * np.eye(X_labeled.shape[1])
    ) @ X_labeled.T
    leverage = np.diag(H)
    
    # Influence score: cook's distance for each new point
    scores = []
    for x_new in X_unlabeled:
        # Leverage of new point
        h_new = x_new @ np.linalg.inv(
            X_labeled.T @ X_labeled + 1e-6 * np.eye(X_labeled.shape[1])
        ) @ x_new
        
        # Influence on each training point
        influence = np.mean(
            residuals**2 * leverage * h_new / ((1 - leverage)**2)
        )
        scores.append(influence)
    
    return np.array(scores)

# === Stopping criteria ===
def should_stop(accuracy_history, window=5, threshold=0.005):
    """
    Stop when accuracy hasn't improved significantly.
    """
    if len(accuracy_history) < 2 * window:
        return False
    recent = accuracy_history[-window:]
    older = accuracy_history[-2*window:-window]
    improvement = np.mean(recent) - np.mean(older)
    return improvement < threshold
